subject: Chemistry
solution:
The Arrhenius equation is given by $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$.
Statement A: The factor $e^{-E_a/RT}$ represents the fraction of molecules having kinetic energy equal to or greater than the activation energy $E_a$. Thus, statement A is incorrect.
Statement B: From the equation, as $E_a$ decreases, the value of $k$ increases, leading to a faster reaction rate. Thus, statement B is correct.
Statement C: For many reactions, the temperature coefficient is approximately 2, meaning the rate of reaction doubles for every $10^{\circ}C$ rise in temperature. Thus, statement C is correct.
Statement D: Taking the natural logarithm of the Arrhenius equation, $\ln k = \ln A - \frac{E_a}{RT}$. Converting to base 10, $\log k = \log A - \frac{E_a}{2.303RT}$. A plot of $\log k$ vs $\frac{1}{T}$ gives a straight line with slope $= -\frac{E_a}{2.303R}$. The statement says slope $= -\frac{E_a}{R}$, which is only true for a $\ln k$ vs $\frac{1}{T}$ plot. Thus, statement D is incorrect.
Therefore, statements B and C are correct.

subject: Chemistry
solution:
By observing the data given, by doubling the pressure, half-life is reduced to half. This result is following second order kinetics. Mathematically it can be proved as follows,

The half-life and initial pressure of nth order reaction can be related as follows,

t12∝Po1-n

t121t122=P011-nP021-n

⇒42=501001-n

⇒2=121-n

⇒2=2n-1

⇒n-1=1

⇒n=2

Hence, the order of the reaction is second order.

$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{K}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{x}}[\mathrm{B}]^{\mathrm{y}} \\ & (\mathrm{I}) 6 \times 10^{-3}=\mathrm{K}[0.1]^{\mathrm{x}}[0.1]^{\mathrm{y}} \\ & (\mathrm{IV}) 2.4 \times 10^{-2}=\mathrm{K}[0.4]^{\mathrm{x}}[0.1]^{\mathrm{y}} \\ & \quad(\mathrm{IV}) /(\mathrm{I}) \\ & 4=(4)^{\mathrm{x}} \\ & \quad \mathrm{x}=1 \\ & \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{K}[\mathrm{A}]^{\mathrm{x}}[\mathrm{B}]^{\mathrm{y}}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { (III) } 2.88 \times 10^{-1}=\mathrm{K}[0.3]^{\mathrm{x}}[0.4]^{\mathrm{y}} \\ & \text { (II) } 7.2 \times 10^{-2}=\mathrm{K}[0.3]^{\mathrm{x}}[0.2]^{\mathrm{y}} \\ & \text { (III)/(II) }\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & 4=2^y \\ & y=2\end{aligned}$
Overall order $=x+y=1+2=3$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
From the given graph, the plot of half-life $t_{1/2}$ versus initial concentration $[A]_0$ is a straight line passing through the origin.

This indicates that $t_{1/2}$ is directly proportional to $[A]_0$, which means $t_{1/2} \propto [A]_0$.

Therefore, the ratio $\dfrac{t_{1/2}}{[A]_0}$ is constant.

Using the coordinates from the graph, we get:

$\dfrac{240}{4 \times 10^{-3}} = \dfrac{x}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$

Solving for $x$:

$x = \dfrac{240 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4 \times 10^{-3}}$

$x = 60 \times 1.5 = 90$

The value of $x$ is $90$.

Answer: $90$

subject: Chemistry
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
For Statement I:
According to the Arrhenius equation:
$\log \left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right) = \dfrac{E_a}{2.303 R} \left( \dfrac{T_2 - T_1}{T_1 T_2} \right)$

Given that the rate constant doubles, $k_2 = 2k_1$. The temperatures are $T_1 = 298$ K and $T_2 = 308$ K.
The gas constant $R = 8.314$ J K$^{-1}$ mol$^{-1} = \dfrac{8.314}{4.2}$ cal K$^{-1}$ mol$^{-1} \approx 1.98$ cal K$^{-1}$ mol$^{-1}$.

Substituting the values:
$\log(2) = \dfrac{E_a}{2.303 \times 1.98} \left( \dfrac{308 - 298}{298 \times 308} \right)$

$0.301 = \dfrac{E_a}{4.56} \left( \dfrac{10}{91784} \right)$

$E_a = \dfrac{0.301 \times 4.56 \times 91784}{10} \approx 12598$ cal/mol $\approx 12.6$ kcal/mol.
Thus, Statement I is true.

For Statement II:
For a first order reaction, the half-life is given by:
$t_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$
This indicates that the half-life of a first order reaction is independent of the initial concentration $[A]_o$. The graph of $t_{1/2}$ versus $[A]_o$ should be a horizontal line parallel to the concentration axis.
The given graph shows $t_{1/2}$ directly proportional to $[A]_o$, which corresponds to a zero order reaction ($t_{1/2} = \dfrac{[A]_o}{2k}$).
Thus, Statement II is false.

Therefore, Statement I is true but Statement II is false.

Answer: Statement I is true but Statement II is false

subject: Chemistry
solution:
According to the Arrhenius equation, $k = A e^{-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}}$.

The given equation is $k = (5.5 \times 10^{11} \text{ s}^{-1}) e^{-\dfrac{28000}{T}}$.

Comparing the exponent of $e$ in both equations:

$\dfrac{E_a}{RT} = \dfrac{28000}{T}$

$E_a = 28000 \times R$

Substituting the value of $R = 8.3 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}$:

$E_a = 28000 \times 8.3 \text{ J mol}^{-1}$

$E_a = 232400 \text{ J mol}^{-1}$

$E_a = 232.4 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, the activation energy is $232$.

Answer: $232$

subject: Chemistry
solution:
Using the Arrhenius equation:

$\log \left( \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} \right) = \dfrac{E_a}{2.3 R} \left( \dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} \right)$

Given:
$k_1 = 1.5 \times 10^3 \text{ s}^{-1}$
$k_2 = 4.5 \times 10^3 \text{ s}^{-1}$
$T_1 = 27^\circ\text{C} = 300 \text{ K}$
$E_a = 60 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} = 60000 \text{ J mol}^{-1}$
$R = 8.3 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}$

Substituting the values into the equation:

$\log \left( \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^3}{1.5 \times 10^3} \right) = \dfrac{60000}{2.3 \times 8.3} \left( \dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} \right)$

$\log 3 = \dfrac{60000}{19.09} \left( \dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} \right)$

$0.48 = \dfrac{60000}{19.09} \left( \dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} \right)$

$\dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} = \dfrac{0.48 \times 19.09}{60000}$

$\dfrac{1}{300} - \dfrac{1}{T_2} = \dfrac{9.1632}{60000} = 1.5272 \times 10^{-4}$

$\dfrac{1}{T_2} = \dfrac{1}{300} - 1.5272 \times 10^{-4}$

$\dfrac{1}{T_2} = 3.3333 \times 10^{-3} - 0.1527 \times 10^{-3} = 3.1806 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$

$T_2 = \dfrac{1}{3.1806 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 314.4 \text{ K}$

Converting to Celsius:
$T_2 = 314.4 - 273 = 41.4^\circ\text{C}$

The nearest integer is $41$.

Answer: $41$

t12∝1A0n-1

21=10.5n-1

n-1=1

n=2

order =2

For the second order reactions half-life is inversely proportional to initial concentration.
